content:
Power without Dharma leads to the ruin of the state. 
—Kautilya, Arthashastra

With nearly 46% of sitting Lok Sabha MPs facing pending criminal cases and over 4,700 cases currently pending against legislators nationwide, the decriminalization of politics is no longer a distant policy goal but an immediate necessity for the world's largest democracy.

In May 2026, I had the distinct privilege of attending the Joint Parliamentary Committee proceedings on the 130th Constitutional Amendment Bill, 2025 as a Research Assistant to Prof. (Dr.) Faizan Mustafa. The Bill is a legislative attempt to answer the crisis. It fundamentally targets the alarming prospect of "governance from jail." Introducing provisions into Article 75 and Article 164 of the Constitution, it mandates the automatic cessation of office for any Minister, including the Chief Minister or the Prime Minister, if arrested and remains in custody for 30 consecutive days for an offence carrying a statutory punishment of five years or more. 

When the political system faces a crisis this severe, the temptation to push through immediate, aggressive legal fixes is high. The mandate should be clear: decriminalization of Indian politics and it must happen now. But, we must clean it up without burning down the foundational principles. A truly "just" approach to an urgent problem requires deliberation. A constitutional amendment alters the structure of our republic, meaning our solutions must be robust. Rushing a Bill by ignoring the exhaustive deliberation risks creating unintended vulnerabilities in our framework. 

The presence of eminent legislators like Asaduddin Owaisi, Aparajita Sarangi, M. Mallesh Babu, Supriya Sule, Ravi Shankar Prasad, Neeraj Shekhar, Devesh Chandra Thakur, Ujjwal Deorao Nikam and others made our presentation more engaging. Their questions deepend my understanding and sharpened my interpretations. 

May the constitutional principles prevail.
